title: The Relationship Between Presence and Financial Healing
body:
The Uncomfortable Truth About Your Money & Your Mind

Truth is, your money problems aren't really about money. They're about presence. Or, more accurately, the *lack* of it. You've been conditioned to chase, to fear, to avoid, to project. Never to simply *be* with your financial reality. And that's where the healing begins. Not in another budgeting app. Not in another get-rich-quick scheme. But in the quiet, often terrifying, space of now. You see, you've spent years, maybe decades, living in a financial future that hasn't arrived or a financial past that won't let go. You're constantly calculating, worrying, planning, regretting. You're rarely, if ever, just *here* with what is. This isn't some woo-woo spiritual concept. This is fundamental. It's the bedrock of any real change. Without presence, you're just reacting. You're running on old programming, old fears, old stories. Think about it. When you're stressed about money, what's happening? You're usually projecting into a terrifying future where you're broke, homeless, or unable to provide. Or you're replaying past mistakes, beating yourself up for decisions you made when you didn't know better. You're rarely sitting with the actual numbers, the actual transactions, the actual choices *right now*. This constant mental time travel drains your energy, clouds your judgment, and keeps you stuck in a loop. Your bank account is a mirror, not a scorecard. It reflects your internal state. It reflects your beliefs, your fears, your habits. When you're constantly avoiding looking at it, or only looking at it through a lens of panic or judgment, you're not seeing clearly. You're just reinforcing the very patterns you want to break.

The Money Wound: It's Deeper Than You Think

The money wound runs deeper than your budget spreadsheet. It's an emotional, psychological, and often spiritual wound. It's imprinted on you from childhood. Brad Klontz, a pioneer in financial psychology, talks extensively about "money scripts" - unconscious beliefs about money that dictate your financial behavior. These scripts aren't logical. They're deeply ingrained narratives passed down through generations, shaped by your earliest experiences. They tell you things like "money is evil," "you have to suffer to make money," "I'll never have enough," or "money will solve all my problems." These scripts operate on autopilot. They drive your spending, your saving, your investing - often without your conscious awareness. When you're not present, you're merely a puppet to these scripts. You're reacting from a place of fear, scarcity, or unworthiness, all stemming from these deep, often unexamined, beliefs. Kate Northrup, in her book Sacred Success (paid link), talks about how your relationship with money is a reflection of your relationship with yourself. You can't hate yourself and expect to have a healthy relationship with money. You can't feel unworthy and expect abundance to flow effortlessly. It just doesn't work that way. The internal state precedes the external reality. Always. So, how do you heal these deep wounds? You start by bringing presence to them. You stop running. You stop distracting yourself. You sit with the discomfort. You observe the thoughts, the feelings, the sensations that arise when you think about money. You don't judge them. You don't try to change them. You just *notice* them. This is the radical act of self-compassion that paves the way for genuine financial healing.

The Illusion of Control and the Power of Observation

You think you need to control everything to fix your money. You chase more income, stricter budgets, perfect investments. And yes, practical steps are important. But without presence, these efforts are often futile. They're like trying to bail water from a sinking boat with a sieve. You're addressing symptoms, not the root cause. The truth is, you can't control the market. You can't control every unexpected expense. You can't control how other people behave financially. What you *can* control is your response. Your attention. Your presence. Krishnamurti, the great spiritual teacher, spoke extensively about observation without judgment. He said that "the ability to observe without evaluating is the highest form of intelligence." This applies directly to your financial life. When you look at your bank balance, do you immediately judge it? Do you tell yourself stories about what it means about you as a person? Do you spiral into anxiety or shame? Or can you simply observe the numbers? Just the numbers. No story. No judgment. This isn't easy. It goes against every instinct you've developed. You've been trained to judge, to compare, to label. But this practice of pure observation is incredibly liberating. It creates space between you and your thoughts, between you and your emotions. It shows you that you are not your thoughts, and you are not your feelings. You are the awareness *behind* them. When you can observe your financial situation without the immediate overlay of fear or judgment, you gain clarity. You see opportunities you missed. You identify patterns you weren't aware of. You make choices from a place of calm discernment, not panicked reaction. This is where true financial intelligence resides. Right?!

Mindful Money Practices: Bringing Presence into Action

So, how do you actually *do* this? How do you bring presence into your daily financial life? It's not about meditating for hours on your bank statements. It's about small, consistent practices. 1. The Daily Check-In: Set aside 5-10 minutes every day. Not to "do" anything. Just to *look* at your money. Open your banking app. Look at your transactions. Don't judge. Don't panic. Just observe. Notice what thoughts and feelings arise. Write them down if it helps. This isn't about solving anything. It's about building awareness. It's about desensitizing yourself to the fear and shame that often surround your money. 2. Conscious Spending: Before you buy anything - anything at all - pause. Take a breath. Ask yourself: "Am I buying this out of true need or desire, or out of habit, fear, or a desire to numb myself?" Is this purchase aligned with my values? With my true self? This isn't about deprivation. It's about intentionality. It's about reclaiming your power from impulsive, unconscious spending. 3. Gratitude for What Is: Even if you're struggling, find something to be grateful for. The roof over your head. The food in your fridge. The fact that you have access to a bank account. Gratitude isn't about pretending everything is fine when it's not. It's about shifting your focus, even for a moment, from scarcity to sufficiency. It's a powerful antidote to fear. 4. Financial Journaling: Write about your money. Not just the numbers, but your feelings about it. Your fears, your hopes, your past experiences. This is a powerful way to bring your unconscious money scripts to the surface. When you see them on paper, they lose some of their power. You can then consciously choose to rewrite them. Vicki Robin, in her seminal work Your Money or Your Life (paid link), talks about calculating your "real hourly wage" and understanding your "life energy." This practice forces you to be intensely present with your choices. It makes you ask: Is this purchase, this job, this investment, truly worth the life energy I'm trading for it? It's a real exercise in financial awareness and presence. Know what I mean?

Discomfort as a Catalyst for Growth

Let's be honest. This process is uncomfortable. Really uncomfortable. You're essentially dismantling years of avoidance and ingrained patterns. You're shining a light into the dark corners of your financial psyche. That light can feel blinding at first. It can bring up shame, anger, regret, fear. But here's the thing: discomfort is where growth happens. Brené Brown, whose work on shame and vulnerability is invaluable, argues that leaning into discomfort is essential for wholehearted living. Her book Daring Greatly (paid link) is a masterclass in this. You can't heal what you're unwilling to feel. You can't change what you're unwilling to acknowledge. Your financial discomfort is a messenger. It's telling you where you're out of alignment. It's pointing to the places where your money scripts are running the show. Instead of running from it, turn towards it. Ask it what it's trying to tell you. This isn't about wallowing. It's about courageous inquiry. When you sit with the discomfort, when you allow yourself to feel the fear without letting it dictate your actions, something shifts. The intensity of the emotion often dissipates. You realize you can *handle* it. You're stronger than you think. This builds resilience. And financial resilience isn't just about having a big savings account. It's about your capacity to meet financial challenges with calm and clarity.

The Long Game: Patience and Compassion

This isn't a quick fix. Financial healing, like any deep healing, is a journey. There will be days you feel great, days you feel like you're backsliding, and days you just want to give up. That's normal. That's human. Patience is key. You didn't develop these money wounds overnight, and you won't heal them overnight. Be gentle with yourself. Treat yourself with the same compassion you'd offer a dear friend who was struggling. Shame is the enemy of healing. Self-compassion is its greatest ally. Celebrate the small wins. Did you look at your bank account today without spiraling? Did you pause before an impulse purchase? Did you acknowledge a fear without letting it overwhelm you? Those are huge victories. They're evidence that you're building new neural pathways. You're rewiring your relationship with money, one present moment at a time. Remember, wealth isn't what you accumulate. It's what you stop fearing. When you release the grip of fear, when you become present with your financial reality, you open yourself up to true abundance. Not just monetary abundance, but an abundance of peace, clarity, and freedom. Does that land?

FAQs About Presence and Financial Healing

What if I feel too overwhelmed to even start?

It's completely normal to feel overwhelmed, especially if you've been avoiding your financial situation for a long time. The key is to start incredibly small. Don't try to fix everything at once. Begin with just one minute a day of presence. Open your banking app, take a deep breath, and just look at one number. Don't judge it, don't try to change it. Just observe. This tiny step builds momentum and resilience. Think of it as stretching a muscle you haven't used in years.

Is this just about positive thinking?

No, this isn't about ignoring your problems or pretending everything is fine when it's not. It's much deeper than positive thinking. Presence is about *awareness*. It's about seeing your financial reality clearly, without the overlay of fear, shame, or outdated beliefs. Positive thinking can be a form of avoidance if it's used to bypass uncomfortable truths. Here, we're leaning *into* the discomfort, observing it, and understanding it, so you can make conscious choices from a place of clarity, not wishful thinking.

How does this connect to practical financial advice like budgeting or investing?

Presence is the foundation upon which all practical financial advice becomes truly effective. Think of it this way: you can have the best budget spreadsheet in the world, but if you're constantly avoiding looking at it, or if you're spending impulsively out of unconscious fear, that spreadsheet is useless. Presence allows you to stick to your budget, make informed investment decisions, and manage debt from a place of calm and intentionality. It helps you identify *why* you're struggling with these practical steps and address the root cause, rather than just treating symptoms.

So, your challenge today: for the next five minutes, sit quietly. Bring to mind your financial situation. Don't do anything. Don't try to solve anything. Just observe what thoughts arise. What feelings come up? Don't judge them. Just notice. Be present with *what is*. It's the most powerful financial step you'll ever take.

Om Shanti Shanti Shanti
meta_description: Discover how a lack of presence impacts your financial well-being. Learn to connect with your financial reality and begin your journey toward lasting financial
author: Kalesh
